data.title: National Energy Services Reunited Targets MENA And Asia's Rapid Industrialization And Urbanization
data.description:
Buy Rating Reflects Positive Sentiment Towards NESR

This analysis aligns with the current market sentiment on the NASDAQ for National Energy Services Reunited Corp. (NESR) stock, up 55% YTD. Wall Street is generally bullish on this stock, as MarketWatch indicates:

NESR stands out in the energy sector as a US-listed company with a strategic position in the Middle East, North Africa, and Asia Pacific regions. Based on this discussion's focus on the dynamic high-growth trends in these regions and the company's financial position, we ultimately expect to see evidence of the company's ability to capture share in these markets, supporting a distinctly bullish sentiment towards NESR shares.

About the Business of NESR

Founded in 2017, NESR is a leading provider of oilfield services in the MENA region (Middle East and North Africa) and the Asia-Pacific region. These services, made possible by a workforce of 7,000 employees in 16 countries, encompass reservoir potential optimization processes, including hydraulic fracturing, cementing, coiled tubing, filtration, completion, and stimulation.

The company also offers drilling and evaluation services such as directional drilling, drilling fluids, wireline services, and rig services, thus supporting its clients in the more efficient development of reservoirs.

NESR's Diverse Service Portfolio

Based on recent business results and trends, the combination of reservoir potential optimization services with well services—the core of NESR's production-oriented offering—plays a more central role in the company's profitability and future growth prospects. While drilling and evaluation services also contribute significantly to these two objectives, they currently play a subordinate role.

NESR's Portfolio by Region: Key Macroeconomic, Geopolitical, and Market Trends

These are the key macroeconomic, geopolitical, and market trends we have identified in each of the two geographic regions where NESR provides its services.

MENA Region

It has long been known that the MENA region (Middle East and North Africa) is among the world's largest oil producers. Anyone who hears or reads about countries in this region, such as Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Kuwait, and Iraq, immediately thinks of oil and its ubiquitous production. The economies of these countries are heavily dependent on oil production, and to maintain this tradition, they rely on services and technologies like those continuously provided by NESR. NESR markets these services and contributes to preserving the MENA region's oil tradition and its strong, charismatic appeal, which persists despite the expansion of renewable energies and, together with them, continues to drive the global economy and growth. One market does not exclude another; this is the principle of an economy whose ultimate goal is to grow through the interconnectedness of markets in a world that, in the face of geopolitical tensions, is finding ever greater reasons for globalization. Amidst this diversity, the opportunity for dialogue arises. Despite inevitable conflicts and friction, this offers an opportunity to improve and strengthen relationships in the long term. Paradoxically, even protectionist tariff policies aim for this goal: they help economies adapt to strategies that best meet constant change—and thereby become more resilient and future-proof, for the benefit of society and the economy. This strengthens domestic and foreign companies, which are increasingly forming stable alliances.

The MENA Region: The Crucial Role of NESR Comes Into Play

Investors who closely monitor all markets are aware of the opportunities arising from the heterogeneity and diversification of resources—for example, the diverse energy generation from renewable sources as well as from oil, natural gas, and fossil fuels. To power an energy-intensive world with its AI data centers, electric vehicles and batteries, electrification, and defense technologies, the MENA regions, with their rich heritage of oil and gas as primary resources, play a crucial role. To secure their strong position in oil production, MENA governments are investing in efficiency improvement programs, including fracking, well stimulation, and advanced reservoir technologies. This is precisely where NESR's production services come into play. This is how the situation appears from an investor's perspective and aligns with the demands of a global economy that must grow while simultaneously becoming more efficient—with maximum results and minimal resource consumption.

Mammoth Agreements in the MENA region

The following are some of the key initiatives to secure the country's oil and gas supply in the region, which raise high expectations for robust demand for the services promoted by NESR:

The United Arab Emirates' unconventional oil and gas reserves, for which a drilling contract worth US$1.7 billion was signed in May 2024, are being developed by three partners—one in the Middle East and two in the USA; similar projects could follow.

Eni and BP, long-standing leading operators in the Egyptian energy sector, have been commissioned by EGPC and EGAS to advance natural gas discoveries in the Mediterranean, including exploration of the Temsah concession area. This opens up growth opportunities for operators like NESR, strategically positioned in the region with a wide range of production and well services.

The Asia-Pacific Region

The second region on which NESR focuses is experiencing rapid industrialization and strong population growth in countries such as India, China, and Southeast Asia, accompanied by rising energy demand. This is particularly true for China, which aims to overtake the US in the global GDP rankings and become the world's leading economy. However, neither China nor the other energy-hungry countries of the Asia-Pacific region can rely too heavily on foreign energy resources, as this would strain their budgets and jeopardize the achievement of their goals. Instead, they must develop their own extensive oil and gas reserves. Global markets, which consider the economic aspects of geopolitics, are favorably disposed toward the scenario of these countries developing their own oil fields, provided this is done with efficient well services and appropriate evaluation methods to optimize production. In exchange for less reliance on imported products, the technologies of Western companies like NESR, based in Houston, Texas, and with a strategic presence in the Asia-Pacific region, would have gained more ground in an environment of exploitation opportunities that has hopefully become fairer for all oil company service providers, both domestic and foreign, as a result of the global trade war initiated by US President Donald Trump.

The Enormous Potential of the Asia-Pacific region

Examples of demand drivers for NESR services: A) The development of reserves in challenging, dense formations for new shale oil and large-scale gas production in the Sichuan Basin, which is of strategic importance for China's energy security. B) Maintaining production increases in mature fields, such as providing additional oil and gas volumes in the offshore areas such as Mumbai and the leading role of the Assam region in the Indian oil industry. C) The drilling operations must penetrate complex geological formations such as those in the Beetaloo Basin, which makes the project technically very challenging due to its unconventional gas resources, but Australia is rich in such rock types.

These are just a few examples of the opportunities that the Asia-Pacific region offers for specialized production services such as NESR's well stimulation and reservoir optimization services.

National Energy Services: Q4 2025 Results, The Situation

Seeking Alpha reports on ACCESS Newswire on February 17, 2026, and reveals NESR's key financial results for Q4 2025 Results:

Revenue was driven by improved capacity utilization and the awarding of new contracts. Adjusted EBITDA, adjusted earnings, and operating cash flow all contributed consistently to the growth trajectory. The adjusted EBITDA margin of 21.2% remained essentially unchanged compared to the previous quarter, meaning that NESR can sustain this expansion without increasing operating costs. Sequential trends show positive momentum, building on year-over-year progress, which is good.

Net profit has not improved compared to previous periods, as it includes non-cash impairment charges on minor technology investments and provisions related to the insolvency of other companies.

Rosy Outlook for NESR

In a comment on NESR's results for the fourth quarter of 2025, Stefan Angeli, Chief Financial Officer, said:

[…] As we enter 2026, our focus remains on executing our growing backlog efficiently, maintaining strong margins, improving working capital performance, and further enhancing returns on capital. We believe NESR is uniquely positioned to enter its next phase of growth with record revenues, strong operational momentum, and a clear focus on delivering durable, long-term value to shareholders.

Shareholder Value, Large-Scale Growth In Various Countries

Sherif Foda, Chairman and Chief Executive Officer, added:

We are very pleased with our fourth quarter results, which cap another year of disciplined execution and strategic progress for NESR. The quarter follows the announcement of several significant contract awards, most notably our integrated unconventional completions scope in Saudi Arabia's Jafurah development, which further reinforces our long-standing partnership with Aramco and our role in enabling world-class unconventional operations in the Kingdom. […]. Importantly, we continue to see strong momentum beyond Saudi Arabia, with new awards across the broader MENA region supporting a multi-year growth trajectory. As we enter 2026, NESR is shifting to a totally different gear and scale, operating from a position of strength, with multiple opportunities spanning across different countries, and a highly committed team. We remain focused on delivering consistent value to our customers, shareholders, and partners, and I am extremely proud of what our people have accomplished.

Non-GAAP free cash flow for the fiscal year ending December 31, 2025, was $120.8 million, compared to $124.2 million in the prior year. This decrease is temporary, as the company is increasing its capital expenditures, which we estimate to be $143.4 million in 2025, compared to $105.1 million in 2024. NESR has identified the right time for growth and is taking advantage of it. If not now, when is the right time, then, to increase spending on growth investments? Certainly not while the business is stagnant. Current opportunities, particularly in the MENA region and the Asia-Pacific region, are promising, and NESR is rightly investing in new equipment and technology to support its service segments growth. Specifically, services to optimize reservoir potential (including hydraulic fracturing, cementing, coiled tubing, filtration, completion, and stimulation) and well services aimed at helping customers develop reservoirs more efficiently. Judging by the share price uptrend, shareholders are confident that these actions will deliver the desired results, including improved annual free cash flow and other profitability metrics that are currently lagging behind annual revenue growth. But if revenue growth is expected to translate into higher profits, and the year-on-year unchanged adjusted EBITDA margins indicate that NESR has costs under control, it is only a matter of time until the next step is that the desired improvements will be visible in other areas, where profitability will also make important strides.

Financial Situation

The previous paragraph suggests that the financial condition is ready to absorb the beneficial inflows. Will this current state of the financial situation—that is, the snapshot as of September 30, 2025 – already be sufficient to support NESR during this phase of very rapid, hyperbolic growth? It is now crucial to maintain a sound, efficient, and competitive financial position, to cover expenses, and to secure all necessary opportunities, because, as CFO Stefan Angeli explains:

During 2025, we continued to prioritize cash generation and debt reduction, ending the year with Net Debt of $185.3 million, down nearly $90 million year-over-year, and a significantly strengthened liquidity position. As we enter 2026, our focus remains on executing our growing backlog efficiently, maintaining strong margins, improving working capital performance, and further enhancing returns on capital.

Total debt (short-term and long-term debt) amounted to US$310.1 million as of December 31, 2025, compared to US$382.8 million as of December 31, 2024.

About the balance sheet, the company stated:

The decrease in Net Debt reflects scheduled long-term debt repayments made during 2025, more than offset by a larger magnitude of free cash flow generation.

Considerations About the Financial Situation

NESR is able to repay its debt on time, which strengthens the market's confidence in the company's financial strength and also positively affects its credit rating, should the company need to raise additional debt to support growth or exploit new opportunities. In relation to these opportunities, look at what is happening in the MENA and Asia-Pacific regions - a very dynamic environment. It cannot be said that the risk is fully assessed in relation to the magnitude of these opportunities that may arise in such a dynamic picture of the MENA region and the Asia-Pacific region, and that NESR may at any given time wish to capture. This picture allows us to imagine a stable but immeasurable growth, a dense growth but fraught with fierce competition. In order to remain and operate dynamically for long enough and seize as many opportunities as possible, it may be necessary to go a little further with risk and adopt a more courageous, entrepreneurial, and adventurous attitude.

Given the outlook for NESR's current financial situation, we believe this provides shareholders with a solid basis to be reasonably optimistic about the repercussions if NESR moves towards a more adventurous way of doing business.

Financial Leverage Ratios

The following information is based on NESR's previous quarterly reports:

Q4 2025 adjusted EBITDA was $84.4 million.

Q3 2025 adjusted EBITDA was $64.0 million.

Q2 2025 adjusted EBITDA was $70.6 million.

Q1 2025 adjusted EBITDA was $62.5 million.

Adjusted EBITDA for the last twelve months (LTM) was $281.5 million.

With debt of $310.1 million, the debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio for the last twelve months was 1.1. This ratio indicates that the company is not over-indebted and that earnings can cover liabilities—a positive signal for investors and lenders. NSER is in a significantly better position compared to many competitors, as GuruFocus indicates a median value of 1.925 for the ratio of net debt to EBITDA for the industry.

NESR's interest coverage ratio (LTM) is 3 (= Seeking Alpha’s LTM NESR’s Operating Income/Seeking Alpha’s LTM Interest Expense), which, while not as high as the industry median of 5.67 reported by GuruFocus, is still well above the minimum tolerable level of 1.5 to 2.0. NESR is therefore able to cover the interest payments due on its outstanding debt.

Although the Altman Z Score of 1.56 (see the “Risk” section on Seeking Alpha) indicates uncertainties in the grey areas, the index, as this analysis shows, is on a good path of improvement and should therefore not be a cause for concern for investors.

NESR National Energy Services Reunited Corp. On The NASDAQ

Shares of National Energy Services Reunited Corp., which trade on the NASDAQ under the symbol “NESR,” were quoted at $4.58 per share, representing a market capitalization of $486.89 million.

Technical Analysis

The stock price has fluctuated between $5.20 and $25.21 per share over the past 52 weeks. The average of this range is $15.205 per share. Currently, NESR stock is trading 59.2% above this 52-week average. The 20-day moving average is above the 50-day moving average, which in turn is above the 100-day moving average, and this is finally above the 200-day moving average. This orderly arrangement of trends on the chart suggests that the market is aiming for positive developments in the short, medium, and long term. The RSI is consistently at 74.16, which is close to overbought territory. The stock price is in this position because market sentiment is continuously pushing it toward this level. Therefore, NESR is not an overvalued stock. Although volatility can cause temporary price declines, the stock, as this analysis shows, offers growth potential.

Valuation

Compared to its direct competitors SLB, Halliburton Company, Weatherford International plc, and Liberty Energy Inc., NESR stock appears cheap in terms of its price-to-cash-flow ratio (TTM).

But measured by the EV/EBITDA (TTM) ratio, it is no longer the cheapest company in the group.

The conclusion regarding the NESR stock assessment is closely related to the first point of the following paragraph on risks, to which we now refer.

The Risks

These are the risks.

Hastily Drawn Conclusions Based On A Detached Assessment Of Financial Indicators

Since the EV/EBITDA ratio is higher than that of comparable companies, but the price-to-cash flow (P/CF) ratio is the lowest, one might hastily conclude that the stock is highly leveraged and that investors are undervaluing equity due to the risk associated with debt. This would explain the low P/CF. The company's total valuation (equity + debt) is high relative to the actual cash flow from its assets, which is reflected in the high EV/EBITDA ratio. This situation suggests that a company in the volatile oil and gas sector—as is often the case—may be exposed to greater risk than its peers during a market downturn. Therefore, the buy signal enters questionable zones.

Aside from the fact that the analysis of NESR's financial situation and the review of its debt ratios have shown that its debt is by no means risky, but rather indicates a stable and manageable debt profile, it strengthens NESR's long-term competitiveness as a service provider in multi-year contracts. The following chart from Seeking Alpha shows that NESR has the lowest debt level. Accordingly, the assumption that the visibly higher enterprise value (EV) compared to competitors, relative to EBITDA, is due to high debt, is incorrect.

The high enterprise value is instead attributable to the increase in NESR's share price on the Nasdaq, which has risen by 55% since the beginning of the year and by 157% in the last twelve months—much faster than comparable companies. Despite this, NESR's stock continues to have the lowest price-to-cash-flow ratio among its peers.

We are convinced that the market has a positive outlook for NESR's future prospects and that the company continues to have growth potential. A low price-to-cash-flow ratio suggests that the stock is still trading at attractive prices on the Nasdaq.

Oil and Gas Price Fluctuations

NESR's share price correlates with the oil and gas industry and its sensitivity to commodity price fluctuations. While the chart suggests this correlation is stronger for crude oil futures (CL1:COM) than for natural gas futures (NG1:COM), it cannot be said that NESR's share price fluctuations on the NASDAQ generally keep pace with commodity price fluctuations. As the chart created on TradingView shows, there are indeed long periods when the two securities move independently.

Rather, it is possible that NESR's share price is less volatile than the fluctuations in futures contracts, leading to less uncertainty in forecasts and some flexibility to adjust positions if new market developments necessitate a revised forecast. But when correlation is positive, a sharp drop in oil prices could prompt oil exploration and production companies to review their investment plans and potentially reduce an investment strategy that, in retrospect, appears disproportionate to the changed market conditions. Reduced spending on exploration and production activities could negatively impact demand for NESR's services, as projects might be postponed or canceled. Before the market realizes the effects of declining revenues and shrinking margins, these could already be priced into NESR's share price.

Instability In Key Markets

Instability in key markets, particularly in countries like Iraq and Libya, could lead to delays or cancellations of contracts, resulting in an undesirable loss of business opportunities. This is particularly true in Libya, which has developed into an area characterized by crime of all kinds.

Competition from larger, established companies does not currently pose a significant risk, as their leverage is no better than NESR's, which will be an advantage for NESR as the MENA and Asia-Pacific regions seek investments to help them develop their resources. But the oil and gas services industry remains highly competitive, and companies like Schlumberger, Halliburton, and Baker Hughes could hinder NESR's efforts to maintain and expand its market share. This would be unfortunate given the very positive forecasts provided by current analyses of future scenarios for the markets in the Middle East, North Africa, and the Asia-Pacific region.

Conclusion

NESR offers promising growth prospects, particularly due to the strong outlook stemming from the potential operational momentum in the MENA region and the Asia-Pacific area. Furthermore, the company can leverage its solid financial position, which, combined with higher utilization rates and the signing of new contracts, creates even more favorable conditions for further revenue growth. Effective debt management with a low net debt ratio (net debt/EBITDA) ensures financial stability. There are also prospects for improved cash flow and higher profitability, as well as disciplined cost management and the expansion of market share in key sectors. Thanks to ongoing expansion opportunities and the positive outlook for the energy services market, NESR is ideally positioned for continued success, making it a compelling buy for investors seeking exposure to the sector.

data.title: Ames National: Spreads Are Widening Again
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Regional banks have benefited from a widening in interest rate spreads after two years of pressure. Ames National (ATLO), a small, Iowa-based community banking holding company, saw its shares up 21% YTD. This is a particularly impressive performance for a slow-compounder with low beta.

This buy rating is based on ATLO's revenue and profitability growth combined with the stock price momentum, as the spread between what ATLO pays on deposits and what it earns on loans widens.

Recent Performance

The growth in ATLO's net income is impressive. Net income rose by 85% in Q4'25, exceeding $6.5 million. ROA almost doubled, reaching 1.25% in Q4'25, up from 0.66% in the same period of the prior year.

Net interest margins improved, rising from 2.38% to 3% as of December 2025. This helped interest income rise by 3.5% to $17.6 million despite the loan portfolio declining to $1.29 billion, down from $1.31 billion in Q4'24.

In other words, ATLO earns more on a smaller portfolio with higher yields.

In recent years, we've seen ATLO's net income become more volatile, as monetary policy changes have become more abrupt. Prior to the pandemic, back to the Great Depression, net income hovered mostly in the $3 to $4 million region. After the pandemic, ATLO reported quarterly net income between $2.1 and $6.7 million.

At the same time, interest expense on deposits dropped to $6.9 million, down from $7.9 million, despite deposits increasing. The decline in deposit expense accelerated in Q4'25, reflecting easing competition for deposits and the impact of the Fed's rate cuts in recent quarters.

Balance Sheet

ATLO's loan portfolio declined slightly in Q4'25 to an average balance of $1.29 billion, down from $1.31 billion in the same period of the prior year. This was partially offset by a decline in Investment Securities to $656.2 million, down from $668.5 million. Overall, total interest-earning assets were down/flattish at $2.04 billion, compared to $2.05 billion in the same period of 2024.

What is interesting is that yields across the loan book rose in Q4'25 across all asset classes except high-yield agricultural loans. Rates on real estate loans, the largest asset class, went up by 33 basis points, reaching an average rate of 5.18% in the quarter, up from 4.85% in Q4'24. Despite the Fed rate hikes, rates on long-term loans haven't fully tracked short-term rates. In other words, the yield curve has steepened. The decrease in deposit costs in Q4'25 reflects these dynamics. The average yield on interest-bearing deposits was down 30 basis points from 2.15% to 1.84% in Q4'25.

The company's results are particularly encouraging given ATLO's exposure to the agriculture sector. Iowa ranks #1 in corn production in the US. In 2025, corn prices ended lower despite an encouraging first half of the year.

In terms of exposure, ATLO has significant exposure to the agricultural sector. Roughly 22% of the company's loan assets are for the agriculture sector.

Challenges in the agricultural sector naturally impacted ATLO's portfolio. Classified assets, which track loans that management deems as having elevated risk, increased to 2.7% of total assets in Q4'25, up from 2.3% just in the prior quarter.

Dividends

The growth in earnings and the widening of spreads came after a period of margin and rate spread contraction. In 2024, ATLO cut its dividend to maintain financial flexibility. Today, a dividend increase is possible. However, management can also decide to maintain the current distribution level, exercising patience before rushing into raising dividends. Historically, ATLO adopted a stable-dividend strategy.

In Q4'25, the board authorized the purchase of 200,000 common shares through November 2026. Management exercised 34,947 of this figure, leaving 165,053 shares left under the buyback program.

Valuation & Outlook

This year, ATLO forecasts EPS between $2.7 and $2.8 per share. That's a significant growth compared to $2.14 reported in 2025. Management cited a continuation of the rate spread tailwinds as funding costs decline while lending income increases, or at least stays flat. Based on 2026 EPS estimates, ATLO trades at a forward P/E ratio of 10.1x, with the share price now trading at $27.9/share.

In terms of valuation, ATLO seems to fall in line with peers' average.

Risks

The primary catalyst for this buy thesis is the widening rate spreads. Management's 2025 net income estimates seem to reflect confidence that the current monetary environment remains accommodative to profits. However, the reality is that spreads can narrow quickly according to monetary conditions.

Although ATLO runs 6 banking institutions, all of its business is concentrated in Iowa. Below are the banks operating under the ATLO umbrella:

  • Boone Bank & Trust
  • First National Bank
  • Iowa State Savings Bank
  • Reliance State Bank
  • State Bank & Trust
  • United Bank & Trust

This regional concentration creates risk. In recent quarters, Iowa, which relies heavily on agricultural exports, was caught up in international trade wars and tariffs, making it harder for farmers to trade their produce. In 2025, agricultural commodity prices also fell. Although ATLO's assets are predominantly real estate, it does have exposure to the agricultural sector. Today's Supreme Court ruling against Trump's global tariffs is constructive to the geopolitical backdrop. However, international trade agreements will continue to impact Iowa's farmers.

Final Thoughts

The difference between when ATLO's banks pay for deposits and what they earn on loans has expanded. This is because deposit rates are set based on short-term rates, while loans are naturally longer-term.

The spread change is a reflection of broader monetary dynamics. The shape of the yield curve has become steeper. A steepening yield curve is often a sign that the market expects short-term interest to fall, while inflation expectations for the future period grow. A bet on ATLO is a bet that these dynamics will continue.

data.title: Vishay Precision: AI Opportunity Keeping The Story Alive, But I'm Remaining Skeptical
data.description:
Vishay Precision Group (VPG) is an industrial technology designer and manufacturer, with its 3 key revenue generators being sensors, weighing solutions, and measurement systems. Sentiment has been negative for VPG post-earnings, with the RSI immediately pulling back from oversold territory as investors seem to dislike the most recent quarter, just after VPG hit an all-time high at the start of the month. I believe that VPG's valuation is now slightly inflated after the recent run up, as the valuation is now slightly less attractive for an entry point despite a ~20% pullback already, but opportunities lie ahead for VPG. Hence, I'm rating VPG at a 'Hold.'

Thesis

There are multiple catalysts for VPG for 2026, with a big part of the next phase of AI being humanoids clearly benefiting VPG with their orders as well as potential margin expansion, which could lead to VPG generating more customers. This tends to lead to a long-term relationship since VPG is a B2B focusing on specialised products, as the stickiness and potential partnerships enforce VPG's moat. An emphasis on specialist sensors on humanoids for 2026 is a huge catalyst going forward, as the TAM for robotics is massive, especially compared to VPG's current size.

VPG has rallied over the last year, with its stock price gaining over 75% despite a recent ~20% pullback directly post earnings. The rally was led with optimism for VPG's future with solid new orders from business development initiatives as well as being undervalued 12 months ago. Valuations are now rather mixed, which doesn't give a good entry point for investors, as the value play last year is no longer really a value play, as valuations are already higher than historical averages. Unless VPG soars to high valuations like the tech sector, which may be possible if their humanoid book-to-bill gains massive momentum. Though VPG isn't really an AI company, which means that it doesn't have AI valuations, a change in sentiment or acceleration in growth regarding AI could lead to higher valuations, but the size of the business is nowhere near enough to justify this, as their target for 2026 for new orders from business development initiatives, which is boosted by humanoid robotics-related orders, is still a small size of their revenue.

Q4 2025 Review And Looking Forward

Q4 reported $80.6M in revenue and adjusted EPS of $0.07 per share, beating analyst top line expectations but missing bottom line expectations of $0.21 per share, a huge 66% miss. Adjusted EBITDA also missed analyst expectations of $7.85M by 23.6%, reporting just $6M in Q4, which is also another huge miss. VPG has now maintained 5 consecutive quarters of at least a 1 book-to-bill, which suggests for now that demand is stable, since they are shipping the same amount to new orders every quarter. This is not necessarily a bad thing since this is signalling that VPG customers are demanding more products for projects, and the potential for acceleration in their bookings is mostly carried by sensors, as a low book-to-bill only gives VPG room to grow as it is already a relatively low number as it gets more exposed to the AI sector.

Sensor bookings are now regaining momentum, though revenue fell sequentially, which is not a big deal as their book-to-bill, which is, in my opinion, a more important trend for future growth, continues to gain momentum and now stands at 1.15, which is important for VPG as sensors is their largest segment of the 3 and stood at 37% of revenue in Q4.

VPG also focuses on different aspects of the humanoid itself, as they mention in their conference with Jefferies:

On customer number one, though, we are designed in, we believe, in terms of our sensors in the joints of the robot. So it's the shoulders, the elbows, the wrist, etcetera. And it and these, sensors are playing a critical role in the functioning of this robot.

The second customer we're now engaged with providing sensors in the hands of the robot. So essentially providing the tactile feel of the robot, enabling that robot to actually perform delicate tasks in one moment and then something, something else in another moment.

Though it isn't really clear just yet which part VPG will have a focus on, VPG is focused on advanced strain gauge sensors, which it already has experience in in many different key industries such as aerospace and defence, automotive design, manufacturing, and engineering. Humanoids are still at a rather early phase, so VPG is still positioned quite well if it continues to build momentum for new orders, as we are still in the stage of R&D where firms are trying to develop humanoids that can actually be brought to market for different use cases. The main question now is whether VPG can continue to gain momentum in 2026, as new customers and orders from new developers are a key thing to look out for.

I believe that this is VPG's most exciting segment, as though VPG doesn't really have exposure to the AI segment, their specialised sensors could accelerate with design wins and new orders from humanoid projects, as humanoids would play a large part in the next phase of the AI revolution. Projections from Morgan Stanley analysts had the TAM for humanoid processors and sensors at $305B by 2045, though obviously still 2 decades away, the size of the TAM should simply show the potential for TAM to capitalise on just sensors. With humanoid orders only being $4M in 2025 and now expanding to their third humanoid developer customer, this could be the start for VPG to establish relationships as it emphasises lifetime relationships for their humanoid sensors, as they mention on their website when describing the StrainBond (3 links above). New customers benefit VPG as their back-and-forth improvements in sensors would lead to better knowledge of customer requirements, and if the competition starts to heat up, increased spending/R&D on humanoid testing would also benefit VPG, increasing their backlog.

I expect continued acceleration in revenue and a larger book-to-bill in Q1 2026, as a higher book-to-bill in Q4 than Q3 likely already suggests growth in revenue from Q4 2025 to Q1 2026, so the main emphasis falls back on book-to-bill in Q1 2026, as the trend and momentum have to be clearer as VPG's metrics across the board have been rather rocky. Humanoid orders are also expected to grow in 2026, with $1M already at the start of the year, as a clearer number in the next report for Q1 will make it easier to estimate new orders for 2026, as January 2026 alone has already achieved 25% of the orders of 2025 alone. I believe the next few reports are very important for VPG as well as being very volatile, as investors try to figure out whether VPG deserves AI valuations and their exposure to AI. Even though the answer is basically no due to their extremely limited exposure, it is still at early stages, and getting in now could be extremely smart if traction accelerates and VPG gains momentum in orders.

Weighing solutions in Q4 stood at 34% of revenue, as it is a relatively stable segment and is also affected by cyclicality. Revenue has declined compared to 2 years ago, with Q4 2023 revenue at $30.4M and Q4 2024 revenue at $25.7M, which has now recovered to $27.7M. There isn't really a catalyst here for VPG, as recovery from cyclicality has been rather weak. As though it is stable, it is very choppy, which means there isn't a real trend looking forward. With book-to-bill now trending upwards, VPG needs to continue this trend to justify its valuation, as a third of the business being choppy is not exactly bullish when only 1/3 segments are only having catalysts while the other 2 continue to remain heavily cyclical.

Measurement systems bookings are currently on a decline at a low book-to-bill similar to the prior year, so though the huge drop seems worrying, it is still higher than the book-to-bill 1 year prior. I believe that the next quarter is very important to determine if what they say actually comes to light, that the timing of projects leads to lower bookings, and if a similar trend will happen like the prior year, where Q4 is slightly weak and it recovers from Q1-Q3. If bookings start to decline in Q1, it will likely suggest that the revenue trend may decline in the short term. If sensors don't continue to outperform expectations and book-to-bill trending upwards, measurement systems weakness will be a huge problem looking forward.

Measurement systems is the only segment for which gross margin remains high for VPG, while the other 2 segments are declining QoQ heavily, so weak booking numbers, which signal lower revenue, suggest weakness for VPG as the other 2 segments margins are contracting.

From the recent earnings call:

In addition, in January, we received a follow-on order from one of our initial 2 humanoid customers of approximately $1 million. 2026 is expected to be a pivotal year for the overall humanoid robotics market as leading companies move decisively from prototype into early production and real-world deployment.

Adjusted gross margin of 37% in the fourth quarter decreased from 40.5% in the third quarter was impacted by $1 million related to unfavorable product mix and $1 million due to inventory reductions. In addition, we incurred approximately $1 million of discrete inventory and manufacturing impacts as well as a $400,000 impact from unfavorable foreign exchange.

Regarding the outlook, for the first quarter of 2026, we expect net revenues to be in the range of $74 million to $80 million, assuming constant fourth fiscal quarter 2025 exchange rates.

On their margins, which is a clear issue in this quarter since they missed EPS by 66%, it was mentioned by management that unfavourable product mix affected margins seen above, totalling $3.4M. As they missed adjusted EBITDA by $1.85M, which is just over half of the affected margin. Next quarter is important to determine whether management was telling the truth and the margin has only been affected for 1 quarter, but a 66% miss on EPS is still a huge miss regardless, especially for a stock that has more than doubled from its 52-week low to its 52-week peak. Contracting margins make it hard to justify valuations, especially as full-year revenue growth is rather stagnant.

Looking forward, their AI exposure needs to continue to perform well to justify valuations, as this quarter mentions follow up orders from old customers, and as management mentions that 2026 is a clear big stage for humanoid robotics, I expect management to shed more light for the next few quarters on potentially new customers or increased capex spending from these developers, which benefits VPG.

They guided for $45M in new orders from business development initiatives, which suggests 18% growth YoY, which is honestly not an extremely bullish number. 2025 beat their expectations by $8M, so taking $45M as a base case and $53M if it outperforms expectations, which would suggest 39% growth YoY, a way more bullish number to go from for investors. If the next few quarters deliver numbers that suggest outperformance of the initial projected number, it would serve as a solid catalyst, as the current number isn't extremely exciting.

Assuming revenue comes in at $77M in Q1, which is the midpoint of the guidance, this would suggest a 7.3% revenue growth YoY for Q1, as VPG's revenue continues to bounce back, as though margins are taking a hit, the bottom line is revenue continues to trend upwards after a couple of weak quarters over a year ago.

Valuation

There was a bull thesis last year that VPG could be seen as undervalued, as VPG's P/S was at 1 or even lower, which is cheap for a firm with solid revenue and cash larger than their current debt pile. However, after this recent rally, VPG's P/S and P/E are now trading at higher than 5Y historical averages, which is slightly worrying as the actual ceiling for how much investors are willing to pay is blurry even after the recent selloff. Though their P/S is lower than the sector median, due to poor and contracting margins, their P/E remains elevated compared to the sector median. Due to this, though it has already sold off, the upside seems limited as the numbers are rather mixed.

If they increase their AI exposure, though, VPG could trade at a higher premium if their orders from humanoid robotics do start to gain momentum, so any headline of new orders and relationships is very bullish for VPG, especially when AI-related news tends to send the stock upwards, so there is a unique point to VPG's current valuation, as a P/S of just under 2 isn't extremely expensive. This obviously makes VPG more volatile, but many robotics humanoid companies tend to trade at extremely high valuations, though the exposure might be small, any momentum or positive sentiment could send the stock upwards.

Final Thoughts

This is only my assumption and belief, but I think that the recent rally was also contributed to by the fact that VPG has tangible assets, as AI fears linger from software and AI could be seen as a bubble, VPG is essentially a safer bet since their revenue is way less volatile compared to the AI sector, causing a rotation in assets. This obviously works both ways since the rally could be lightened by their small AI exposure, but the fact is with small exposure and essentially selling a product that is hard to be displaced once locked in, like humanoid sensors, since it takes time for developers to test if sensors work and changing to a different sensor would take time and potentially be costly, so VPG's AI exposure is slightly different as momentum is only starting to gain.

A scenario to further clarify is semiconductor manufacturing machine manufacturers, where in theory their demand will fall one day when the supply of chips is higher than the demand, which is not the case by a long shot right now. With VPG's AI exposure being robotics humanoids, assuming it becomes successful in actually providing these sensors, the point where demand falls would potentially be decades away, as we are still in the extremely early stages of robotics. From this point of view, VPG is way safer, and if there is a rotation into tangible assets, VPG will likely continue to gain amongst AI fears.

For now, I'm holding off, as VPG still has 2 potentially struggling segments, which is around 2/3 of the revenue, while 1 segment is thriving, and though it is thriving, it doesn't carry enough weight of the other 2 segments to give me investor confidence in the near term until further positive sentiment and catalysts surrounding robotics arise.

data.title: Vita Coco's Stock Is Still Too Expensive After The Pullback
data.description:
The coconut-water company Vita Coco (COCO) offers investors high growth at a high price. The last time I wrote about it, I said it was fairly valued, but tariffs were a risk factor. Now, after the removal of tariffs on coconut water, Vita Coco is likely to have higher margins and higher EPS in 2026. Vita Coco also reported weak Q4 2025 results, as expected, but this stock still has high-growth potential if you look past its choppy quarter-to-quarter performance. But even after its recent pullback, it doesn’t seem like a great deal right now.

Vita Coco reported 0.4% growth in Q4 2025, but its full-year 2025 revenue growth was 18%, and I think that gives investors a better perspective about its long-term growth potential. With $609.8 million in full-year revenue, Vita Coco also reported $222.6 million in gross profit for a gross profit margin of 36.5%. Its gross profit margin was 38.5% in 2024. So, now that the coconut water tariff is gone, Vita Coco is positioned to deliver another year of double-digit revenue growth while maintaining higher margins. And Vita Coco’s guidance confirms this viewpoint.

Vita Coco’s Full-Year 2026 Guidance Implies Strong Margin Growth

In its Q4 2025 report, Vita Coco guided for revenue of $680 to $700 million in 2026, which is equivalent to 11.5% to 14.8% revenue growth. In my previous article, my valuation was based on a long-term growth rate of 12.9%. At the midpoint of its guidance, Vita Coco is projecting 13.2% revenue growth, so its guidance is a little bit higher than I originally expected, which is a good sign.

Vita Coco also expects to have a gross margin of 38% in 2026, which is a 150 basis point increase from 2025. I think this margin growth might already be priced in, though, because Vita Coco’s stock rallied in November after the tariff news came out. Additionally, Vita Coco guided for adjusted EBITDA of $122 million to $128 million in 2026. At the midpoint of both its revenue and adjusted EBITDA estimates, the company’s margin would be 18.1%. In my previous article, I used a margin of 15.7% to project the company’s 2026 results. So, it looks like the removal of the coconut water tariffs made a big difference.

The Impact of the Tariffs Isn’t Fully Gone Yet

Even though the tariffs have been removed, Vita Coco still has to include them in its expenses until it sells all of its current inventory. On the Q4 2025 call, Vita Coco President Michael Kirban said, “We still have the residual impact of the 2025 tariffs in our inventory, which means we will not see the long-term cost of goods representative of our ongoing business until Q2.” So, Vita Coco’s 2026 guidance includes the impact of tariffs in Q1 2026. And it’s possible that its margins could improve further in 2027 because of this factor, which would justify slightly higher multiples.

Vita Coco is a beverage company, so the obvious move for an analyst would be to compare it to other beverage companies. And I did that last time as well. But like I said last time, soda and energy drink companies often have gross margins above 50%, and even with the tariffs gone, this company’s gross margin will be around 38%. So, Vita Coco is still likely to trade at a lower P/S multiple than other beverage companies.

Vita Coco Is Making Up for Lost Private-Label Business

Vita Coco also makes private-label coconut water for a large club store. The company didn’t identify this customer in its report, but it’s likely that it’s Costco (COST). Last year, the club store found another coconut water supplier and stopped ordering from Vita Coco in specific regions. As a result, Vita Coco’s private-label coconut water sales fell 30% to $63 million in Q4 2025. Because of the timing of this deal, Vita Coco will also report lower private-label revenue in Q1 2026.

The company is now making up for this loss by selling more coconut water at Walmart (WMT) instead. Walmart has been stocking more wellness products and selling more health food, so it’s more willing to stock niche products like coconut water now. It sounds like this partnership is working, and Walmart is selling more of Vita Coco’s coconut water. On the call, Vita Coco CEO Martin Roper said, “I think we said in the prepared remarks, Walmart is adding 5%, 6% to our total scans right now, which is very cool. And given that growth rate, Walmart is gaining share of the Coconut Water category as a retailer.”

Vita Coco’s margins could also improve further because of this transition. The company is replacing private-label coconut water sales with branded sales at Walmart, and branded products typically have higher margins.

Rising Competition Is a Risk for Vita Coco

Vita Coco is a niche health-food manufacturer that’s currently the leader in a rapidly-growing market. While its revenue growth and margins might look good right now, this company may be vulnerable to both startups and private-label manufacturers. So, it’s a bad sign that a major customer replaced Vita Coco as a supplier. While stores like Costco typically don’t identify their private-label suppliers, Costco did start selling coconut water from Once Upon a Coconut in January. Harmless Harvest and C2O are other popular coconut water brands. Last time, I said I wasn’t concerned about competition from the big beverage companies, but private-label brands and coconut water startups may pose larger threats.

Updated Comparative Valuation for Vita Coco

It looks like Vita Coco’s stock price rose in November because investors now expect it to report better bottom-line results. So, I’ll use the EV/EBITDA ratio in this update. On February 19, Vita Coco had a forward EV/EBITDA of 21.13. On the same date, Celsius (CELH) had a forward EV/EBITDA of 21.74, and Monster (MNST) had a forward EV/EBITDA of 30.10. So, Vita Coco is trading at a high valuation, but so are its peers. Vita Coco also has a forward EV/sales ratio of 3.86 versus 5.34 for Celsius and 9.60 for Monster, but that’s because it has lower margins than the other beverage companies.

Updated 2026 Projection for Vita Coco

Because Vita Coco has choppy quarterly results, last time I used its long-term CAGR to estimate its growth rate. It sounds like it may report weak results in the first quarter and much stronger results in the second half of 2026. After updating my CAGR estimate with this company’s 2025 results, it now has a long-term CAGR of 13.6%. So, I’ll project that this company’s revenue will rise 13.6% from $609.8 million to $692.7 million in fiscal 2026. With an 18.1% adjusted EBITDA margin, Vita Coco would report $125.4 million in EBITDA.

With a 2026 EV/EBITDA of 21, this company would have an EV of $2.633 billion. After subtracting $15 million in debt and adding $204 million in cash, it would have a market cap of $2.822 billion. According to Vita Coco’s Q4 2025 report, the company currently has 64.2 million shares outstanding. After dividing a market cap of $2. billion by 64.2 million shares, I get a one-year target price of $43.96. Vita Coco’s current stock price is $51.76, so its current price is 15% higher than my one-year target price.

Conclusion

Now that the coconut water tariff is gone, Vita Coco is likely to have higher margins in 2026 than I expected when I wrote my previous article. But it looks like investors also bought this stock after the news came out in November, so even after the recent pullback, Vita Coco is still an expensive stock. While Vita Coco is performing great right now and its partnership with Walmart appears to be working well, I think its stock is slightly overvalued right now and isn’t a good deal. So, I maintain my hold rating on Vita Coco.

data.title: Scotiabank: Further Upside Is Capped By Valuation
data.description:
Thesis

I rate Scotiabank (BNS) as a Hold, mainly due to valuation. The Price/book has actually turned out to be a very sound metric to value this company, since it oscillated between 0.9-1.9x historically. My base case for fair value is the historical median at 1.4x. This is primarily because that's where the market traded in the last 10 years. The USD P/B ratio is currently at 1.52x, but as I discuss in the valuation section, I expect it to compress to 1.47x. Regarding ROE, while there are different ways to calculate it (GAAP vs. Non-GAAP, quarterly vs yearly, total equity-based vs common equity-based), they range between 8-12.5%. After all, this is the annual return for the shareholder, and it justifies, to some extent, the mild overvaluation. Plus, I believe it will increase a bit (say 100 bps) due to cost reductions plus accretive divestitures, but this might not be enough to justify a buy rating given the current valuation.

There are several key drivers for this company to consider. The first is the wealth management and investment banking segments, which have been showing stellar growth (19% and 50% Y/Y). This is mainly due to the rallies of the Canadian and Chilean stock markets. However, after these impressive rallies, it's time to get more grounded regarding the outlook, since valuation is at higher levels. Another important driver is its LATAM segments. While they're riskier, they have a younger population and a growing middle class, which has allowed them to outpace the growth rate of the Canadian segment. Lastly, the company is making divestitures and planning cost reductions in order to improve profitability. However, while this improvement is worth highlighting, the potential increase in ROE might be just between 100 and 200 bps. In other words, any potential multiple compression in the P/B ratio will be marginal.

On the flip side, the main restraint I see is the sluggish Canadian banking sector (which is down 9% Y/Y), especially due to its high exposure to the real estate sector. The current high debt levels of households can cap future growth.

Company Overview

'Bank of Nova Scotia,' or better known as "Scotiabank," is one of the 'Big 5' banks in Canada, though some people talk about the 'big 6.' As of December 2024, it was the third-largest bank ranked by revenue.

The Canadian banking segment currently (FY 2025) accounts for 44% of its total income, while in FY 2024 it accounted for 47.8%. Also, this segment was down 9% Y/Y. This was weighed down by an increase in provision for credit losses (PCLs) by $4.7 billion, representing an increase of 8% Q/Q and 10% Y/Y. As shown below, it was a poor fiscal year but, counterintuitively, a great quarter, where quarterly revenues grew 15% Y/Y, and quarterly diluted EPS grew at 35% Y/Y. In my opinion, this explains and justifies, to a great extent, the current rally.

A substantial share of its operations is in LATAM countries and , to a lesser extent, in the US. These countries accounted for this double-digit growth in both revenues and earnings. Regarding LATAM, it might have some drawbacks compared to its Canadian/Developed market peers, but it also has two benefits. First, it has a younger demographic overall. For example, the median age in Canada is 41.5 years, while Mexico's, Peru's, and Chile's are 30.3, 30.8, and 37.5, respectively. In Canada, fewer than 15.1% of people are under 15 years old, while in Mexico, this number jumps to 24.5%. In short, this is a direct proxy for long-term growth, given that this younger population is the bank's future customer base.

The second aspect is the growing middle class in LATAM, which creates new clients and expands the addressable market for Scotiabank. These two factors explain why some LATAM segments outperformed in the growth rate compared to the Canadian one. However, there were some exceptions in Central America and in Colombia, which led the company to divest from them. I believe this was the way to go. For example, ROE in the Canadian segment is 17.8%, Mexico's is at 17.8% as well, and Chile's is a more modest 13.3%. On the other hand, Colombia's was a poor 3.3%, and Central America was just at 1.3%.

Regarding these divestitures, while they were/are accretive, they didn't change the pro forma ROE much as of now. For example, FY 2025 ROE was 11.8% while the pro-forma ROE is 11.9% (p.25). However, there are two things to consider here: first, we shouldn't dismiss small improvements, since every bit counts to the bottom line. Second, there are expenses linked to this divestiture procedure. So, we might expect a bit more (even though not much) of an accretive impact in the following quarters.

Segments

Regarding the segments, there's a stark difference in growth rates between the bank segments and the wealth management/investment banking ones. The former have been struggling to grow, especially the Canadian banking segment. In fact, it has been decreasing in the last 8 quarters. Also, while this segment accounted for 48% of net income in Q1 24, now it's accounting for just 37%. The wealth management and investment banking, though small, are growing at 19% and 50% quarterly Y/Y.

That said, we should keep in mind that the Canadian and Chilean stock markets had a pretty good year recently, surging 30% and 54%, respectively. This has been spicing up a bit in these wealth management and investment banking segments, since when the markets go up, people tend to get excited and invest/trade more. However, after this kind of rally, we should moderate our outlook on it, expecting a more modest performance ahead.

Also, the gold rally of the last 12 months might have indirectly helped this company, since it's usually considered a key bank linked to miners, and it has a specialized division for that industry. Regarding this, I'm more bullish, since the effect of rising gold prices might be more sticky. This is because gold prices lead miners to make more money, so they get more eager to increase production, increase M&A activity, and borrow more money.

Outlook

Management aims to hit a double-digit growth in EPS in 2026. In fact, the analyst estimate for 2026 FY (ending in October) is $5.90, which suggests an implied Y/Y growth of 16.30%. They plan to achieve this by doing three things. First, they expect a normalization in provision for credit losses [PCL] driven mainly by the Canadian segment. In the last quarter, it sat at 43 bps, but they expect to drop it to the high 30s. Second, they expect to increase their net interest margin, as they expect interest rates to drop slightly in 2026. Keep in mind that if rates drop, they can reprice the rate they pay to depositors lower, so they'd reduce their costs. On the other hand, having fixed-rate loans, those yields are already locked in at higher levels.

Lastly, they expect EPS to slightly increase via operating leverage, mainly by reducing costs. This is planned to be achieved by reducing headcount, closing physical branches, and automating workflows as they integrate AI across their operations.

However, that said, even achieving this improvement in EPS, it still might be modest, as analysts expect Y/Y growth rates between 11% to 16%, over the next 4 quarters.

Also, we should consider loans to the private sector in Canada; they are slightly slowing down. In the left image is the total amount, and in the right one, its Q/Q variation. While they are acknowledging this by their 'value over volume strategy,' it is still a key headwind for the company. While I consider this strategy a wise move given the current situation for the company, the problem is that it caps upside. While you can reduce costs once or twice, but not indefinitely, sooner or later, you'll need to grow in total volume.

Valuation

One of the most important metrics to value a bank, setting aside price/earnings, is price/book. Now, this ratio is at levels that preceded sharp drops in prices ahead (e.g., 2013-2014, 2017-2018, January 2022, and now). So, that alone should be a reason enough to keep an eye on it. Both the historical mean and median since 2014 are roughly at 1.4x, which I'd call a 'reasonable' level for this company. At the same time, we shouldn't ignore the ROE, which, according to Prof. Damodaran, is the companion of the P/BV ratio.

As shown in the chart below, P/B and ROE move in tandem, indicating a strong correlation between them. That said, ROE has been in a downtrend since 2013, dropping from 16% to 8-9% currently, but improved a bit in the last quarter. We can gather that P/B levels will depend a lot on forward ROE.

Please, consider for a moment the chart above, and let's assume a stock price and price/book ratio at 20% or even higher; how does it fit in the historical chart? While the current ratio is at 1.69x, that assumed ratio would be 2.02x (1.69*1.2). This level has not been seen since 2014. Could it hit it? I believe it actually could (because of momentum), but how reasonable is it to buy expecting that? That's why I'm on the sidelines now. Also, it's important to highlight that the last three rallies in this 2014-2026 period were preceded by low price/book levels, say at 0.8-1.2x or so. For example, the current rally started on April 9th, 2025, at $42.97 and a P/B of 1.04x. In other words, the current rally of 75% has been grounded and well-justified. But the story is completely different at the current valuation.

When comparing it to its peers, the company is a bit overvalued, as indicated in most ratios, except dividend yield. Forward non-GAAP PEG suggests it is at fair value.

Now, when considering a regression analysis between Price/Book and ROE, for diversified banks, it suggests a mild overvaluation. The regression line indicates 1.29x as the fair value (now it's 1.52x). However, this regression analysis should be taken with a grain of salt, as it's not intended to be conclusive. But I like to see valuation from multiple points of view, and it gives a pretty good idea of what the fair value could be, comparing it to other diversified banks. Additionally, this includes emerging market banks, but regarding them, I consider that they're a two-sided issue. On the plus side, they usually have higher growth rates, but the catch is that they are in more volatile economies, which makes their stock prices just as volatile.

Lastly, regarding price/book, there are two additional things to consider. First, there's a difference in the metric depending on whether we look at it in Canadian dollars (CAD) vs. U.S. dollars (USD). The reported book value in CAD was $62.22 per share as of 31 October. At the current TSX stock price of BNS:CA at $104.16, it gives us a P/B ratio of 1.67x. By contrast, the book value in USD is $50.10, and given the NYSE stock price at $75.90, results in a P/B ratio of 1.51x. This explains the difference between the Seeking Alpha's ratio and the YCharts one. Both are different but valid ways of seeing the same thing; however, looking at both at the same time gives us a more complete picture.

Second, regarding the Forward price/book, something doesn't quite line up there. The current ratio is at 1.52x while the Fwd one is at 1.6x; the forward ratio should be lower due to the sustainable growth rate. Considering a ROE of 8.58% and a payout rate of 60.77%, book value should increase at 3.3% Y/Y. ((1-0.6077)*0.0858). So, the Fwd P/B ratio should be roughly 1.47x (1.52/1.033 = 1.47). Obviously, different assumptions about ROE and payout rate will spit out slightly different target ratios, but within that range.

Conclusion

The stock returned an impressive 56% in the last 12 months—that's 48% in price gains plus dividends. When looking at the last 24 months, the total return is around 100%. Also, since this is a high dividend payout company (60%~), it's important to highlight the divergence between stock price and total return (price + dividends). However, over the last 10 years, every time that price/book hit the current levels, the total return has stayed flat or even dropped over the following months.

Switching gears for a moment, someone with a more bearish-leaning view could ask for a downside case. However, I'm far away from the bearish camp, since I see four problems with the bearish thesis. First, the company is slightly improving profitability by reducing costs and making divestitures. This could drive net income a bit higher, which could justify a 'mild overvaluation.' This is because an improvement in earnings makes us more 'eager' to forecast earnings at higher rates ahead. Second, valuation is not 'too overvalued' enough to justify a bearish thesis. Third, momentum/trend is very bullish. Betting against this upward trend might not be wise. Fourth, while I'm concerned about potential sell-offs ahead, the current dividend yield acts to some extent as a buffer.

Wrapping up, I stated why I'm not a bull and certainly not a bear. I believe the company, despite its headwinds in the Canadian banking segments, is doing well overall. However, current valuation leads me to the sidelines.

data.title: Why TTM Technologies Is A Hold Despite AI Powered Growth
data.description:
TTM Technologies (TTMI) is not a very obvious company for investors to get excited about because of its history as an electronics manufacturing services (EMS) or contract manufacturer of printed circuit boards (PCBs), RF components, and electromechanical components. The market has traditionally viewed PCB manufacturers as cyclical, commodity companies. However, although PCB manufacturing still is subject to economic cycles and raw material costs, TTM now differentiates its products by manufacturing PCBs for industries that require more complex designs than those the company previously served.

For example, TTM now primarily serves the Artificial Intelligence ("AI") and Aerospace & Defense (A&D) markets. New Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") Edwin Roks stated on the company's fourth-quarter fiscal year ("FY") 2025 earnings call:

From a demand standpoint, we expect healthy tailwinds due to our participation in 2 key megatrends currently driving economic growth, artificial intelligence and defense. As stated previously, approximately 80% of our net sales are related to these 2 megatrends.

The market is currently revaluing TTM from a cyclical PCB manufacturer to a high-growth AI and Defense infrastructure stock. As a result, the company's stock price is up 247.1% over the last year compared to the S&P 500's (SPX) 11.80%

However, before existing investors add new money or new potential investors initiate one, they should recognize that the market is well aware of this stock and that it is no longer a "hidden gem." In fact, some argue that there is a considerable "hype" premium built into the stock's valuation. Caution is warranted with this one. However, there is enough potential upside that existing investors should consider holding it.

Building electrical boards for the next generation of AI

Some of this discussion may be difficult to understand for those unfamiliar with electronics. I tried to simplify the discussion, while remaining technically accurate, which is difficult.

Let’s look at how PCBs have changed over time. Initially, manufacturers made PCBs to connect electrical components without using bulky, manual wiring, which helped save space. Standard PCBs with trace widths of 50 to over 250 microns (6 to 10+ mils) work well for most consumer electronics, home appliances, basic car systems, and industrial equipment. Trace width refers to the width of the copper lines on a board that carries electrical signals.

Over time, devices became smaller and more complex, requiring the device manufacturers to fit more electrical components into less space. This led to High-Density Interconnect (HDI) boards, which use much thinner traces, about 3 to 10 mils (75 to 250 microns) wide. Cellphones are a good example: as phones went from large, heavy models in the 1980s and 90s that could only be used for phone calls to today’s much lighter, smaller devices that can perform many more functions, like GPS location and internet connectivity, HDI became necessary.

New hardware for the AI era, such as High‑end servers, switches, and data-center backplanes (think of backplanes as the spinal cord of a network), introduced additional challenges that required another solution. Hardware enabling AI requires a network that can deliver very clean, high-speed data to the destination at the right time, as well as deliver a massive amount of electricity to power the GPU (Graphics Processing Unit) or AI Accelerator.

TTM Technologies and other companies met these needs by stacking more layers in PCBs. You can think of each layer as a floor in a building. Boards with many layers are called High Layer Count (HLC) boards. Some uses, like high-end AI server motherboards, combine HDI and HLC technologies. For example, a high-end AI server using NVIDIA (NVDA) DGX A100 or H100 systems might have a motherboard with 18 to over 30 HDI layers. On the other hand, networking and telecom equipment often need thicker, non-HDI HLC boards to keep electrical signals clean at very high data speeds. These boards can have 60 to 80 layers.

When two high-frequency electrical sources are placed too close together in an application, such as a switch, backplane, or router, they can generate signal noise in the other boards or circuits. Designers limit this potential interference by increasing the separation between layers, inserting insulating layers, adding copper shielding, and increasing the number of layers. As a result, backplanes and switching fabrics have many more layers than motherboards. Some boards used in routers or backplanes may have up to 80 HLC thick-layer boards, and that number is about to go even higher. On the earnings call, CEO Roks discusses HLC soon going beyond 100 layers:

The number of layers is going up. There are numbers, let's say, beyond the 100 layers already, which are required. Of course, these systems become more and more and more compact that requires more layers and more complexity. So -- and we are well positioned. We are happy to see that because we are well positioned to do these multiple layers.

Since very few global manufacturers have the specialized equipment or expertise to profitably manufacture a 100-layer board, TTM has a technical moat in HLC to protect its margins.

Now, let's discuss Ultra High Density Interconnect (UHDI) boards.

As AI systems get smaller and include more features, the industry is moving past HDI to UHDI, which uses trace widths thinner than 2 mils (50 microns). TTM uses UHDI for advanced AI modules, leading‑edge military electronics, and extremely compact, high‑performance systems. Fewer than 10 companies worldwide can make UHDI boards, and TTM is the largest U.S.-based manufacturer. The company is increasing UHDI production at its Syracuse, New York, facility. It expects to see its first revenue from this site in the second half of 2026.

Management also reiterated during the earnings call that they expect overall board production to grow by 15 to 20 percent over the next three years, thanks to expansions in both China and the U.S. The market has awarded the stock a premium valuation primarily on expected growth over the next three years from its 22 to 24 global manufacturing sites, including seven in Asia, and on the ramp-up of UHDI production from its Syracuse plant.

Financially solid

TTM Technologies reported "beat and raise" fourth-quarter and fiscal-year 2025 results, released on February 4, 2026, driven by demand for AI-related data center computing and networking, as well as an aerospace and defense (A&D) backlog.

The company beat analysts' fourth-quarter revenue estimates by 2.84% and non-GAAP earnings per share ("EPS") estimates by 2.9%.

For the first quarter of 2026, TTM's guidance of $790 million at the midpoint was higher than analysts' estimate of $732.09 million, suggesting 22% revenue growth if achieved. After the earnings report, analysts increased their estimates to $787.31 million. Management also guided first-quarter 2026 EPS to $0.67 at the midpoint, above analysts' $0.61 estimate, which would mean 34% EPS growth if met. Analysts later raised their EPS estimates to $0.67.

The company reported fourth-quarter 2025 earnings on February 4, 2026. The stock price opened at $87.05 on February 5, a 9.5% decline from the December 4 close.

The company had a big run-up coming into earnings, reaching a 52-week high of $111.00 on February 3. Given the AI hype, TTM may have risen to a level where it's "priced for perfection." Although the company delivered a "beat and raise" quarter, fourth-quarter results were far from perfect, causing a sell-off as investors engaged in profit-taking.

One concern for the market was that cash from operations in the fourth quarter dropped 27% year over year to $63 million. Free cash flow ("FCF") also fell 64% to $12 million. When earnings rise, but FCF declines, it can signal different issues. The most positive explanation is that the company is making major investments in future growth, such as buying equipment well in advance of when the investment will produce returns. Bullish investors usually accept lower FCF if it can create long-term value.

A more bearish view is that working capital is worsening, with higher accounts receivable and inventory and lower accounts payable. The market sees this as a warning sign, as it may indicate falling demand, late customer payments, poor inventory management, or even inflated sales from channel stuffing.

An even worse view is that earnings quality may be slipping and could be unsustainable. This could happen if one-time gains are boosting earnings, if stock-based compensation ("SBC") is high, if depreciation schedules are too aggressive, or if capitalized costs are hiding real expenses.

Since the stock had an elevated valuation, investors adopted a shoot-first, ask-questions-later approach going into earnings and initially sold the stock after seeing the drop in cash flow. However, after reviewing the results and listening to the conference call in greater depth, investors concluded that the company is simply investing in future growth, namely by investing in existing manufacturing facilities in Dongguan and Guangzhou, China, by purchasing additional equipment, preparing those facilities for increased production, and fine-tuning production workflows to maximize efficiency. These production lines currently manufacture artificial intelligence boards.

TTM provided 2026 capital expenditure ("CapEx") guidance of $240 million to $260 million, with expectations of growth in 2027. The company spent $273.94 million in CapEx in 2025, which was 9.4% in sales. This substantial spending was directed toward preparing the production sites in Penang, Malaysia, and Syracuse, NY.

The good news for investors is that the 2026 CapEx guidance indicates a decline from 2025 results, suggesting the company may have moved past peak CapEx spending and could soon enter the investment-harvesting phase.

Still, investors should keep an eye on TTM's free cash flow. If it doesn't improve by late 2026 or early 2027, after the company finishes upgrading the Syracuse and Penang facilities, investors might see fast EPS growth and weak FCF as a sign of lower earnings quality, which could lead to a sell-off.

Another issue that could have caused the stock's initial drop at the February 5 opening and may prove a headwind to its rise moving forward is margin pressure. Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") Daniel Boehle said on the earnings call, "[The Penang facility] Q4 at the gross profit level still had a headwind of about $180 million, and we had guided $160 million, so a little bit worse than expected there in Q4." This worse-than-expected gross margin headwind increases investor concern about execution risk and may cast doubt on management's ability to provide accurate guidance.

Lastly, on February 2, two days before the company released its quarterly results, director Thomas T. Edman sold 16,800 shares of TTM Technologies. Although the stock sale was under a 10b5-1 plan, the market often disfavors company representatives selling stock when the stock is either trading at a high valuation or has made a big move higher. The stock sales likely put pressure on the stock price before and after earnings.

Despite some possible issues, the company has sufficient upside from the expected ramp-up in production of UHDI and HCL boards that it may still be worthwhile to hold the stock. As a result, the stock partially recovered during the day after its disappointing open on February 5, closing down 3.78%.

TTM Technologies ended the fourth quarter of 2025 with $501.23 million in cash and short-term investments and $912.34 million in long-term debt.

The company's debt-to-equity ratio is 0.5, indicating that it conservatively finances its operations with equity rather than debt. TTM Technologies has net debt of $525.2 million and adjusted EBITDA of $456.304 million. The company's long-term debt of $912.336 million plus short-term debt of $3.815 million from its balance sheet within its fourth-quarter and 2025 earnings release equals a total debt of $916.151 million. Net debt equals total debt of $916.151 million minus cash and equivalents of $501.234 million, for a net of $414.917 million.

Dividing a net debt of $414.917 million by an adjusted EBITDA of $456.304 million, as shown on the company's fourth quarter and 2025 earnings release, equals a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 0.91, which gives the company plenty of "dry powder" to fund its expansion plans without needing to take on dangerous levels of new debt. The company has a current ratio of 1.9, which is excellent for a manufacturing company and indicates that it has sufficient current assets to meet short-term obligations.

Valuation

TTM's trailing-12-month ("TTM") price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is 3.299, well above its three-, five-, seven-, and ten-year medians. When a company trades at a premium to its median, it may signal overvaluation. However, in this case, the company is fundamentally changing from a slow-growth commodity business to a high-growth business with a technological moat. As long as the market expects TTM to benefit from the tailwinds of AI growth, it is unlikely to return to a sub-1.0 P/S ratio. However, if AI doesn't benefit TTM as much as the market expects, the stock could decline sharply.

The following compares TTM's forward P/S, P/S/G, TTM FCF margin, gross margin, operating margin, and return on invested capital ("ROIC") to its electronics/EMS/industrial‑tech peer group. The market generally considers a company with a higher P/S or P/S/G ratio to be overvalued relative to companies with lower ratios.

Company name FY 2027 forward P/S ratio FY 2027 forward revenue growth FY 2027 P/S to estimated growth (P/S/G) TTM FCF margin TTM Gross margin TTM operating margin TTM Return on invested capital ("ROIC")

Advanced Energy Industries (AEIS) 5.25 13.23% 0.40 7% 37.7% 9.3% 12.9%
